title: How koalas escaped a genetic bottleneck
html_body:
What matters in science |
View this email in your browser Friday 6 March 2026

Hello Nature readers,
Today we learn how koalas escaped a genetic bottleneck, see the first ‘half Möbius’ carbon-based molecule and meet a mathematician modelling Mexico’s drug cartels.

If you enjoy this newsletter, please consider recommending it to a friend or colleague. Click here to forward it by e-mail. Thank you!
In the 1920s, there were only between 500 and 1,000 koalas in the Australian state of Victoria after they’d been killed for their fur in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. (VCG via Getty)

How these koalas came back from the brink

Koalas (Phascolarctos cinereus) in Victoria, Australia recovered from a severe genetic bottleneck through an increase in recombination — a process in which the DNA from two parents gets shuffled to form new sequences in their offspring — during a rapid population expansion. Researchers found that the number of individuals that breed and contribute to the next generation’s gene pool has jumped substantially in the past few decades, despite previously collapsing by more than 90%. The findings suggest that even species pushed to the brink of extinction can recover lost genetic diversity.

Nature | 4 min read
 Reference: Science paper

Protein-clearing cells break in Alzheimer’s

Tanycytes — specialized cells that clear toxic proteins from the brain — malfunction in people with Alzheimer’s disease, which leads to the build-up of abnormal tau proteins that characterizes the condition. From samples of blood and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), researchers found that less tau had moved from the CSF to the bloodstream in people with Alzheimer’s compared with people who did not have the disease, which suggested that tanycytes weren’t working properly. They then looked at post-mortem tissue samples and found that tanycytes were destroyed or fragmented in people with Alzheimer’s.

Nature | 4 min read
 Reference: Cell Press Blue paper

A Möbius molecule with a new twist

Chemists have synthesized a new type of carbon-based molecule with an unprecedented twist in its structure. The team calls the looped molecule a ‘half-Möbius’, inspired by the Möbius strip — a twisted loop with one continuous surface. In the half-Möbius molecule, the chain of atoms is twisted by 90° to make the loop, instead of the full 180º seen in a standard Möbius strip. The molecule can exist in two versions depending on whether it twists left or right. The versions differ in chirality, meaning that they are mirror images of each other.

Nature | 4 min read
 Reference: Science paper

International Women’s Day 2026

How and why to filter men out of the data

Across many conditions, women’s health is uncharted scientific territory. Until the 1990s, women were rarely included in clinical studies and female animals were excluded from studies using model organisms because of a prevailing belief — now discredited — that hormonal fluctuations made their biology too complicated. Disaggregating data by sex is a powerful way to help fill in the blanks — but researchers say it’s not used enough.

Nature | 16 min read

The missing pieces of menopause science

“We’re not good at treating perimenopause because we don’t completely understand it,” says endocrinologist Susan Davis, who has studied menopause for decades. The hormonally turbulent years leading up to a woman’s final period have been neglected by science compared with postmenopausal studies, which focus on life after a year without menstrual bleeding. That’s left a knowledge gap that frustrates providers and their patients. “I can tell you these treatments are helping my patients relieve their symptoms,” says gynaecologist Rachel Pope. “But I can’t tell you from our current data what that means several decades from now.”

Nature | 17 min read

The women who inspire inspiring women

Six winners of three Nature-sponsored awards — the Estée Lauder Companies’ Inspiring Women in Science award, the Sony Women in Technology award and the John Maddox Prize — name women whom they find inspiring themselves. The nominated individuals include Anna Abalkina, a research-integrity scholar who had to relocate from Russia to Germany, and Onikepe Owolabi, who published a landmark study of post-abortion care in ten countries.

Nature | 13 min read

Read more in Nature’s collection for International Women’s Day.

Features & opinion

Using maths to crack Mexico’s drug cartels

Mathematician Rafael Prieto-Curiel and his colleagues have created a mathematical model that revealed Mexico’s powerful drug cartels are the country’s fifth-largest employer. The model suggests that the government’s current strategy could lead to more casualties, more incarceration — and bigger cartels. “That result is one of the darkest results I have ever had in my research career,” says Prieto-Curiel. But finding ways of preventing people from joining organized crime groups could cut casualties and reduce cartel size — if they can be successfully implemented in the real world.

Nature | 14 min read

Futures: All the world is staged

A dedicated scientist must follow the call of the wild in the latest short story for Nature’s Futures series.

Nature | 6 min read

Five best science books this week

Andrew Robinson’s pick of the top five science books to read this week includes a campaigning guide to alternative meats, a history of the groundbreaking Montreal Neurological Institute and a stimulating analysis of the many paradoxes that are raised by the development of AI tools.

Nature | 4 min read

Quote of the day

“It underscores how important natural history collections are. We never know how we might apply them.”

By comparing samples of moss recovered from a crime scene in a cemetery with those kept in the collection of the Field Museum in Chicago, plant scientist Matt von Konrat found evidence that helped to solve a case involving more than 100 grave robberies. (The Guardian | 4 min read)
Reference: Forensic Sciences Research paper

In today’s penguin-search puzzle, Leif Penguinson is exploring the exposed bed of the Mayo-Louti river in Cameroon during a dry spell. Can you find the penguin?

The answer will be in Monday’s e-mail, all thanks to Briefing photo editor and penguin wrangler Tom Houghton.

This newsletter is always evolving — tell us what you think! Please send your feedback to briefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Flora Graham, senior editor, Nature Briefing
With contributions by Jacob Smith

items.title: How these koalas came back from the brink
items.snippet: Koalas ( Phascolarctos cinereus ) in Victoria, Australia recovered from a severe genetic bottleneck through an increase in recombination — a process in which the DNA from two parents gets shuffled to form new sequences in their offspring — during a rapid population expansion. Researchers found that the number of individuals that breed and contribute to the next generation’s gene pool has jumped substantially in the past few decades, despite previously collapsing by more than 90% . The findings suggest that even species pushed to the brink of extinction can recover lost genetic diversity.

items.title: Protein-clearing cells break in Alzheimer’s
items.snippet: Tanycytes — specialized cells that clear toxic proteins from the brain — malfunction in people with Alzheimer’s disease , which leads to the build-up of abnormal tau proteins that characterizes the condition. From samples of blood and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), researchers found that less tau had moved from the CSF to the bloodstream in people with Alzheimer’s compared with people who did not have the disease, which suggested that tanycytes weren’t working properly. They then looked at post-mortem tissue samples and found that tanycytes were destroyed or fragmented in people with Alzheimer’s.

items.title: A Möbius molecule with a new twist
items.snippet: Chemists have synthesized a new type of carbon-based molecule with an unprecedented twist in its structure. The team calls the looped molecule a ‘half-Möbius’ , inspired by the Möbius strip — a twisted loop with one continuous surface. In the half-Möbius molecule, the chain of atoms is twisted by 90° to make the loop, instead of the full 180º seen in a standard Möbius strip. The molecule can exist in two versions depending on whether it twists left or right. The versions differ in chirality, meaning that they are mirror images of each other.

items.title: How and why to filter men out of the data
items.snippet: Across many conditions, women’s health is uncharted scientific territory. Until the 1990s, women were rarely included in clinical studies and female animals were excluded from studies using model organisms because of a prevailing belief — now discredited — that hormonal fluctuations made their biology too complicated. Disaggregating data by sex is a powerful way to help fill in the blanks — but researchers say it’s not used enough.

items.title: The missing pieces of menopause science
items.snippet: “We’re not good at treating perimenopause because we don’t completely understand it,” says endocrinologist Susan Davis, who has studied menopause for decades. The hormonally turbulent years leading up to a woman’s final period have been neglected by science compared with postmenopausal studies, which focus on life after a year without menstrual bleeding. That’s left a knowledge gap that frustrates providers and their patients. “I can tell you these treatments are helping my patients relieve their symptoms,” says gynaecologist Rachel Pope. “But I can’t tell you from our current data what that means several decades from now.”

items.title: The women who inspire inspiring women
items.snippet: Six winners of three Nature -sponsored awards — the Estée Lauder Companies’ Inspiring Women in Science award, the Sony Women in Technology award and the John Maddox Prize — name women whom they find inspiring themselves. The nominated individuals include Anna Abalkina, a research-integrity scholar who had to relocate from Russia to Germany, and Onikepe Owolabi, who published a landmark study of post-abortion care in ten countries.

items.title: Using maths to crack Mexico’s drug cartels
items.snippet: Mathematician Rafael Prieto-Curiel and his colleagues have created a mathematical model that revealed Mexico’s powerful drug cartels are the country’s fifth-largest employer . The model suggests that the government’s current strategy could lead to more casualties, more incarceration — and bigger cartels. “That result is one of the darkest results I have ever had in my research career,” says Prieto-Curiel. But finding ways of preventing people from joining organized crime groups could cut casualties and reduce cartel size — if they can be successfully implemented in the real world.

items.title: Futures: All the world is staged
items.snippet: A dedicated scientist must follow the call of the wild in the latest short story for Nature ’s Futures series.

items.title: Five best science books this week
items.snippet: Andrew Robinson’s pick of the top five science books to read this week includes a campaigning guide to alternative meats, a history of the groundbreaking Montreal Neurological Institute and a stimulating analysis of the many paradoxes that are raised by the development of AI tools.

summary.overview: 이번 네이처 브리핑은 코알라의 유전자 병목 탈출, 알츠하이머 치료 관련 신호 세포 연구, 새로운 분자 구조 개발, 성별 데이터 분석의 중요성, 수유기 과학의 부족, 여성 인재의 영향력, 수학을 활용한 멕시코 마약단속 모델, 퓨처스 시리즈의 창의적 이야기, 그리고 주간 최고 과학도서 추천 등 다양한 주제를 다루고 있습니다. 각 기사가 과학적 혁신과 사회적 문제 해결에 대한 통찰을 제공합니다.
highlights: 코알라의 유전자 병목 탈출: 빠른 인구 증가로 재조합 증가로 유전 다양성 회복
알츠하이머 치료 관련 탄이세포 기능 장애 발견: 타우 단백질 축적 원인 규명
반도체 구조를 영감으로 한 '반-모비우스 분자' 합성: 왼쪽/오른쪽 체리성 구조 차이
성별 데이터 분석의 필요성 강조: 여성 건강 연구의 과거 간과된 영역
수유기 과학의 연구 부족: 전후기 연구 균형 부족으로 치료 개발 난관
여성 과학자들의 영감을 주는 인물 추천: 연구윤리학자, 의학자 등 다각적 성공 사례
수학 모델로 멕시코 마약단속 전략 분석: 조직범죄 확산 방지 방안 제시
퓨처스 시리즈: 자연을 향한 과학자의 탐험 이야기
주간 최고 과학도서: 대체육 가이드, 뇌 연구 역사, AI 역설 분석 포함
summary.significance: 이번 뉴스레터는 생물학적 회복력, 신경퇴행성 질환 치료, 분자 구조 혁신, 성평등 과학 연구, 노화 과학, 여성 리더십, 범죄 예방 수학 모델, 창의적 과학 서사, 과학 소통 등 다양한 분야에서 핵심 이슈를 다루며, 과학이 사회적 문제 해결과 인류 발전에 기여하는 방식을 보여줍니다. 특히, 생태계 보존, 의학 혁신, 성평등 확대, 데이터 윤리 등 현대 과학의 사회적 책임을 강조합니다.
covered_item_titles: How these koalas came back from the brink
A Möbius molecule with a new twist
How and why to filter men out of the data
The missing pieces of menopause science
The women who inspire inspiring women
Using maths to crack Mexico’s drug cartels
Futures: All the world is staged
Five best science books this week

article_briefings.title: How these koalas came back from the brink
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 호주 빅토리아 주의 코알라들이 유전자 간극을 극복하며 인구를 회복한 과정에서 재조합 증가가 중요한 역할을 했다고 연구자들이 밝혔다. 이는 두 부모의 DNA가 교차하여 자손에 새로운 유전자 조합을 형성하는 과정에서 인구 확장이 촉진되었음을 의미한다.

article_briefings.title: Protein-clearing cells break in Alzheimer’s
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 알츠하이머병 환자의 뇌에서 독성 단백질을 제거하는 탄시세포가 기능을 상실해 비정상적인 타우 단백질이 축적되는 것으로 나타났습니다. 혈액과 천자액 샘플을 분석한 연구에서 타우 단백질이 천자액에서 혈액으로 이동하는 양이 줄어들어 단백질 제거 기능의 손상이 확인되었습니다.

article_briefings.title: A Möbius molecule with a new twist
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 화학자들이 새로운 탄소 기반 분자를 합성해, 모비우스 띠를 기반으로 한 '하프 모비우스' 구조를 만든 것으로 알려졌습니다. 이 분자의 원자 사슬은 90° 회전하여 루프 형태를 형성하며, 단일 표면을 가진 독특한 구조를 보입니다.

article_briefings.title: How and why to filter men out of the data
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 여성의 건강 연구는 과거 임상 실험에서 거의 배제되었으며, 호르몬 변화로 인해 생물학적 복잡성이 있다고 여겨진 오랜 믿음이 이를 방해했습니다. 이로 인해 여성의 건강에 대한 과학적 이해는 여전히 미개발된 영역입니다.

article_briefings.title: The missing pieces of menopause science
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 수십 년간 연구가 부족했던 경호전기(Perimenopause)의 복잡한 호르몬 변화로 인해 여성들이 효과적인 치료를 받지 못하고 있으며, 이는 경호전기보다는 경호후기(Postmenopause)에 대한 연구가 더 활발하다는 점에서 비춰진다.

article_briefings.title: The women who inspire inspiring women
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 세 가지 Nature 후원 상인 에스티 로더 컴퍼니의 '사이언스에서 영감 주는 여성상', 손니 기술 여성상, 존 매드록상 수상자들이 자신을 영감 주는 여성들을 선정했습니다. 이 중에는 연구 무결성 학자인 앤나 아바르킨이 포함되었습니다.

article_briefings.title: Using maths to crack Mexico’s drug cartels
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 수학자 라파엘 프리에토-쿠리엘과 팀이 수학적 모델을 통해 멕시코의 마약 조직이 국가의 다섯 번째로 큰 고용주라고 밝히며, 정부의 현재 전략이 사망자 수 증가, 구금자 증가, 더 큰 조직 형성으로 이어질 수 있다고 경고했다.

article_briefings.title: Futures: All the world is staged
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 최신 Nature의 Futures 시리즈 단편 소설에서는 과학자가 야생의 부름을 따르며 자신의 열정을 실현해야 한다는 메시지를 담고 있다.

article_briefings.title: Five best science books this week
article_briefings.briefing_ko: Andrew Robinson이 이번 주에 추천하는 5권의 과학 서적은 대체 고기 캠페인 가이드, 몬트리올 신경학 연구소의 역사, AI 도구 개발로 인한 역설 분석 등을 포함합니다.

raw_metadata.subject: How koalas escaped a genetic bottleneck
